# For Educators {#sec-for-educators .appendix}

If you teach, you have probably already been thinking about how the ideas in this book apply to your students. How do you design assessments that reward conversation over delegation? How do you build rubrics that distinguish genuine AI-assisted thinking from copy-paste submission? How do you create classroom environments where AI use is transparent and productive?

Those questions deserve more than an appendix. They deserve their own book.

*Partner, Don't Police: Designing Assessment for the AI Era* is a companion volume that applies the "Conversation, Not Delegation" philosophy directly to teaching and assessment design. It covers rubric frameworks, assignment structures, integrity conversations, and practical strategies for educators across disciplines. Where this book asks "how should I think with AI?", that book asks "how do I help my students think with AI?"

You can find it at [github.com/mborck/partner-dont-police](https://github.com/mborck/partner-dont-police).

If you are an educator reading this book for your own professional development, everything here applies to your work as written. The techniques, frameworks, and habits are discipline-agnostic and role-agnostic. Use them. Then, when you are ready to redesign how your students engage with AI, the companion book picks up where this one leaves off.
